title: I-V characteristics of SNS junctions with a multivalley normal region
authors: Liam Bonds, Shiang-Bin Chiu, Anton Andreev, Boris Spivak
abstract: In multivalley conductors the inter-valley relaxation time $τ_v$ and the inelastic relaxation time $τ_{in}$ may be significantly longer than the intra-valley momentum relaxation time $τ$. We show that this separation of time scales has dramatic effects on the I-V characteristics of SNS junctions with a multivalley normal region. We generalize the Larkin-Ovchinnikov equations describing superconducting kinetics to the case of multivalley superconductors. We use this generalization to obtain a kinetic description of multivalley SNS junctions. We find that at constant voltage bias $V$, the current $I(V)$ is nonmonotonic; it exhibits two peaks of similar magnitude $I_\text{max,1} \sim I_\text{max,2}$ at $V_1 \sim \hbar(eτ_{in})^{-1}$ and $V_2\sim \hbar(eτ_v)^{-1}$, which may greatly exceed the critical current $I_c(T)$. At constant current bias $I$ we find that in a wide interval, $I_c(T) < I \lesssim I_{\text{jump}}$, the nonlinear resistance of the junction is controlled by the long relaxation times and may be several orders of magnitude smaller than the normal state resistance.
summary: The paper provides a theoretical description of SNS junctions where the normal metal has multiple conduction band valleys. It predicts that the separation of different relaxation time scales leads to unique nonmonotonic current peaks and highly nonlinear resistance.
main_problem: Investigating how a hierarchy of relaxation time scales (intra-valley, inter-valley, and inelastic) affects the I-V characteristics of SNS junctions with a multivalley normal region.
main_result: The I-V characteristic is nonmonotonic, exhibiting two current peaks at voltages corresponding to the inter-valley and inelastic relaxation rates, and showing a sharp voltage jump in current-biased regimes.
method: Generalization of the Larkin-Ovchinnikov equations using the Keldysh-Gor'kov-Nambu formalism, Usadel equations, and a zero-dimensional approximation for the junction.
model_or_system: SNS (Superconductor-Normal-Superconductor) junctions where the normal region is a diffusive multivalley conductor, such as n-doped silicon or germanium.
key_observables: Current-voltage (I-V) characteristics, current peaks (I_max), critical current (I_c), and nonlinear resistance.
important_parameters: Relaxation time hierarchy (tau, tau_v, tau_in), Thouless energy (E_T), and voltage scales (V1, V2).
main_assumptions: Diffusive regime, adiabatic approximation for slowly varying order parameters, and a zero-dimensional approximation for the low-bias regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a sketch of a multivalley SNS junction with scattering processes; Figure 2 plots average current vs. bias voltage; Figure 3 shows the I-V characteristic with a distinction between low-bias and high-bias regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem, develops a generalized kinetic framework for multivalley systems, analyzes voltage-biased junctions to find current peaks, and then investigates current-biased junctions to identify the voltage jump regime.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of a multivalley SNS junction with an n-doped silicon (Si) normal region. The orientation of the six Fermi pockets relative to the device geometry is shown at left. The double-arrowed arcs indicate the scattering processes associated with the relaxation times τ and τv.

Figure 2: Plot of average current I versus bias voltage V in an SNS junction with a multivalley normal metal. At bias voltages of the order eV1 ∼τ −1 in and eV2 ∼τ −1 v there exist two maxima of the average current; Imax,1, Imax,2. These maxima are of the same order and may greatly exceed the temperature-dependent critical current Ic(T).

Figure 3: I-V characteristics of current-biased SNS junctions obtained by solving the model in Appendix C. (a) Plot of dimensionless voltage eV/ET versus dimensionless current eI/GNET for T/ET = 4. Black dots are the solutions to Eqs. (C6) and (C7) with 1/(τinET ) = 10−5 and the gray dots are the solutions with 1/(τinET ) = 10−4. The high current asymptotic, Eq. (C9) is plotted in red. The jump current is Ijump = GNE2 T /32eT, which for the chosen parameters occurs at eIjump/GNET ≈0.0079. (b) The same plot on a log-linear scale.
